about: The brand primitives that lynox paints on more than one surface: this product UI,
and the lynox.ai marketing site. Same brand, different codebases — so the values
are necessarily duplicated. This file is the contract that keeps the duplication
honest, and it is the ONLY thing that can: the site is a standalone Astro app and
must not depend on this package (that would drag a Svelte UI into a marketing site
and couple its build to core releases).
If you change one of these values in app.css, `pnpm tokens:contract` (from the repo
root) fails — here, in this repo, on your commit. That is the point. Update this file
in the same change, and know that the other surface has to follow.
DARK ONLY. The light ramps are deliberately NOT shared: the product is a tool you
sit in for hours (flat, quiet, neutral-grey ink); the site is scanned in twenty
seconds (tonal depth, violet-tinted ink). Two named contexts, not a bug.
This file names no other repository and no private path. It is a list of colours.

notGuarded.lyx-bg-overlay: Modal/backdrop scrim. Pure #000 here, near-black ink on light. The other surface has no equivalent — it scrims with its own tonal grounds.
notGuarded.lyx-accent-disabled: Derived, not declared: color-mix(var(--lyx-accent), var(--lyx-bg-muted)). It follows the two tokens above it automatically, so there is nothing to keep in sync.
notGuarded.lyx-focus-ring: The other surface has no focus-ring token. Nothing to converge onto yet.

shapeAbout: Colour was never the whole of it. Both surfaces already agree on the corner radii —
4 / 6 / 8 — and have since before anyone wrote it down, which is exactly the kind of
agreement that drifts the first time someone rounds a card by eye. Now it is stated.
Theme-independent: a radius has no light and dark.
typeAbout: Only the FIRST family is the brand. The fallback stacks legitimately differ — this
surface carries the longer chain (ui-sans-serif, system-ui) because it is an app that
must render before its webfont lands; the other one ships the font with the page.
So the contract holds the family and leaves the stack alone.
productLightAbout: This surface's OWN light ramp. It is NOT shared — that is the whole point of it, and the
reason it is worth writing down. White ground, neutral-grey ink, a darker accent: chrome you
sit in for eight hours should recede. The other surface's light is violet-tinted, because a
page being scanned can afford a mood.
It is recorded here, and guarded here, so that anything that DRAWS this surface — a design
system, a preview, a mockup — can show it truthfully instead of borrowing the site's light
and lying about it. A preview that paints the app in the site's colours is worse than no
preview: it is a wrong answer with a house style.
alsoNotShared: The LIGHT ramps of every colour above — see DARK ONLY in `about`. They are recorded under `productLight`, which is a record, not a contract with the other side.

The semantic SURFACES on the other surface (success/error fg+bg+border triplets, still hard-coded there). Only the three standalone FILL colours — success/danger/warning — are shared, and they are in `primitives`.
--font-serif. The italic serif accent is a MARKETING device — one word, for emphasis, on a page that is scanned. It has no place in a tool you sit in for hours, and this surface does not define it.

The SPACING, TYPE and ELEVATION scales. This surface has none of its own: it rides Tailwind's defaults (text-xs and text-sm alone account for ~900 of its class uses). The other surface authors twelve spacing steps, five type steps and six elevations, because it is scanned in twenty seconds and needs the rhythm. Density here is deliberate, not drift — the same reasoning as the light ramps. Naming them would be inventing a shared decision that was never made.
